reason: Closed-book solvability failure: options A and F are labeled Correct but cannot be concluded from the stem plus general engineering reasoning. A requires paper-specific empirical directionality (HF higher in safety venting than thermal runaway and its temperature-mechanism attribution), and F requires paper-specific threshold behavior at low heat flux/0% SOC (venting without progressing to runaway). These are not implied by the stated conditions alone. Therefore the item depends on hidden literature details.

Closed-book solvability failure: at least one Correct-labeled option (C) requires paper-specific quantitative results (97.4% laminar burning velocity reduction and 99.7% net heat release reduction with 5% DMMP at equivalence ratio 1.0). These exact magnitudes cannot be derived from the stem plus general engineering reasoning, so the item depends on hidden literature details.

Closed-book solvability failure: options A, C, D rely on paper-specific quantitative/empirical findings (e.g., laminar burning velocity/flame temperature being highest at 100% SOC; CO2 >60% below ~25% SOC; HF higher during venting than during runaway). These conclusions are not derivable from the stem plus general engineering reasoning alone, and the stem does not provide the needed composition or emission relationships.

Closed-book solvability failure: the prompt asks for specific SOC-dependent gas composition fractions (e.g., CO≈50%, H2≈25%, CO2>60% at 20% SOC, inert≈20%, elevated C2H4) and specific explosion-limit behaviors (Z-shaped curve dominance by H2; third explosion limit ordering and pressure/temperature shift driven by C2H4). These are paper-specific findings not provided in the stem, so an examinee without the papers cannot uniquely derive or justify the required claims beyond speculation.
